name: Sefirot
shortDescription: The Sefirot are the ten emanations of God in Kabbalah, representing different aspects of the divine nature and serving as channels through which God interacts with the world.
description: The Sefirot (Hebrew: סְפִירוֹת‎, singular: Sephirah סְפִירָה) are the ten attributes or emanations in Kabbalah, through which Ein Sof (the Infinite) reveals itself and continuously creates both the physical realm and the chain of higher metaphysical realms (Seder hishtalshelut). The term Sefirot is derived from the Hebrew word 'sapir' meaning sapphire, suggesting a radiant and illuminating quality. The Sefirot are understood not merely as attributes of God, but as dynamic, interconnected forces that shape reality. They are often depicted as a tree, known as the Tree of Life, with the Sefirot arranged in a specific configuration. Each Sephirah represents a different aspect of God, such as Wisdom (Chochmah), Understanding (Binah), Kindness (Chesed), Severity (Gevurah), Beauty (Tiferet), Victory (Netzach), Splendor (Hod), Foundation (Yesod), and Kingdom (Malchut). Keter (Crown) is the first Sephirah and represents the divine will or intention. The Sefirot are not separate entities but rather different facets of a unified divine reality. Understanding the Sefirot is crucial for comprehending the Kabbalistic worldview and for engaging in spiritual practices aimed at uniting with the divine. The arrangement and interaction of the Sefirot also provide a framework for understanding the structure of the universe and the nature of human consciousness. Their influence extends to all levels of creation, from the most sublime spiritual realms to the mundane physical world. The concept of Sefirot is central to Kabbalistic prayer, meditation, and ethical behavior, as practitioners strive to embody and manifest the divine qualities represented by each Sephirah. The Sefirot are associated with various symbols, colors, and angelic beings, further enriching their symbolic significance.

sources.title: The Wisdom of the Zohar: An Anthology of Texts
sources.author: Isaiah Tishby
